questionText: You are performing a calculation for a single-family dwelling service. The dwelling contains one 14 kW household electric range (240V) and one 5 kW household electric clothes dryer (240V). What is the calculated feeder neutral demand load for these two appliances?
explanation: First, calculate the line demand. The 14 kW range exceeds 12 kW by 2 kW, so the base 8 kW is increased by 10% (5% per kW) to 8.8 kVA. The dryer is calculated at 5.0 kVA (Table 220.54). The total line demand is 13.8 kVA. NEC 220.61(B)(1) permits a 70% demand factor for the neutral of ranges and dryers. Therefore: 13.8 kVA x 0.70 = 9.66 kVA.
sparkyTip: Ranges and dryers get a 70% neutral demand factor. Calculate the full line demand first, then multiply by 0.70 for the neutral.

questionText: A commercial building has a calculated demand load of 90,000 VA. The service is 208Y/120-volt, 3-phase, 4-wire. What is the minimum size XHHW-2 Aluminum service entrance conductor required?
explanation: Step 1: Calculate amperage: I = VA ÷ (V × √3) = 90,000 ÷ (208 × 1.732) = 90,000 ÷ 360.25 = 249.8A. Step 2: Select conductor from NEC Table 310.16, Aluminum 75°C column (even though XHHW-2 is rated 90°C, terminations are limited to 75°C per 110.14(C)). 300 kcmil Al at 75°C = 230A (too small). 350 kcmil Al at 75°C = 250A (sufficient for 249.8A).
sparkyTip: Don't get tricked by the 90°C insulation rating! Terminations limit you to the 75°C column per 110.14(C). Always size aluminum conductors from that column — 350 kcmil Al = 250A at 75°C.
